title: Shoulder Dystocia
chief_complaint: Pushing for 2 hours. Fetal head delivered but retracts back against perineum (turtle sign). Shoulders not delivering with gentle downward traction.

physical_exam.Obstetric: Turtle sign present. Head delivered but retracting. Gentle downward traction fails to deliver anterior shoulder. Shoulder dystocia diagnosed.
physical_exam.Fetal: Fetal HR 90 bpm (bradycardia - head compression, cord compression).
physical_exam.Maternal: No history of shoulder dystocia. No diabetes. Fundal height 42cm (macrosomia).

hidden_data.risk_factors: No diabetes (tested negative at 28 weeks). Prior deliveries: 2,500g and 2,800g. Excessive weight gain in pregnancy (25kg).
hidden_data.fetal: After maneuvers: Delivered at 14:35 (3 minutes). Apgars 4/7/9. Birth weight 4,350g. Erb-Duchenne palsy (right arm - C5-C6 injury).

correct_diagnosis: Shoulder Dystocia with Fetal Macrosomia and Erb-Duchenne Palsy
key_learning_points: Shoulder dystocia = failure of shoulder delivery after head with gentle downward traction. Obstetric emergency. Incidence 0.5-1.5% of vaginal deliveries. Unpredictable (50% have no risk factors)

Risk factors: Macrosomia (>4,000g - 5-10% risk, >4,500g - 10-25%), Maternal diabetes (even with normal birth weight - fetal shoulder:head ratio increased), Prior shoulder dystocia (10-15% recurrence), Prolonged second stage, Operative vaginal delivery (vacuum/forceps), Maternal obesity
Turtle sign (head retracts against perineum after delivery) = pathognomonic. 'Head bobbing' as baby attempts to breathe but shoulders stuck

HELPERR mnemonic (maneuvers in order): H = Help (call for help - OB, NICU, Anesthesia). E = Evaluate for episiotomy (generous episiotomy or proctoepisiotomy to increase space). L = Legs (McRoberts maneuver - hyperflex thighs onto abdomen, flattens sacrum, rotates pelvis - resolves 40%). P = Pressure (suprapubic pressure - NOT fundal pressure - by assistant, pushes anterior shoulder under pubic symphysis). E = Enter maneuvers (internal rotation): Rubin II (push posterior shoulder toward fetal chest), Woods corkscrew (rotate posterior shoulder 180°), Reverse Woods. R = Remove posterior arm (sweep posterior arm across chest, grasp hand, deliver). R = Roll patient (Gaskin maneuver - hands and knees position). Last resort: Clavicle fracture, Zavanelli maneuver (push head back in, emergent C-section), Symphysiotomy

Complications: Fetal: Brachial plexus injury (Erb-Duchenne C5-C6 most common - 80-90% resolve spontaneously by 1 year), Klumpke palsy (C8-T1 - rare), Clavicle/humerus fracture, Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy, Death. Maternal: Postpartum hemorrhage, 3rd/4th degree perineal tears, Uterine rupture, Psychological trauma

Documentation critical: Time of head delivery, Time of shoulder delivery, Maneuvers used and order, Which shoulder was anterior, Cord gases, Apgars. Always debrief with family. Shoulder dystocia is a major cause of litigation
